relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus teaches beside the sea from a boat as the crowd gathers on land.
Parables become his way of carrying deep truth.
The first command is to listen: the sower goes out.
Some seed is lost on the path.
Some springs quickly in shallow ground.
Without root, heat withers it.
Some is choked by thorns and bears no fruit.
Good soil receives, increases, and bears fruit.
Ears must become true hearing.
Those close to him ask about the parables.
The mystery of the Divine reign is given to those drawn inside.
Seeing and hearing can remain closed when the heart refuses turning and release.
This parable becomes a key to the others.
The sower sows the word.
Without understanding, the word can be quickly taken from the heart.
Rocky ground receives with immediate joy.
Without root, pressure causes stumbling.
Thorn-ground hears the word.
Age-worry, wealth-deception, and scattered desire choke fruitfulness.
Good soil hears, receives, and multiplies.
A lamp is meant for the stand, not for hiding.
What is hidden is moving toward revelation.
Again the call is hearing.
The measure of attention shapes what can be received.
Receptivity grows; closedness shrinks capacity.
The Divine reign grows like seed cast on ground.
Growth unfolds while the farmer sleeps and rises.
The earth bears fruit in stages.
When fruit is ready, harvest comes.
Jesus searches for another image of the reign.
The mustard seed begins in tiny hiddenness.
It grows into shelter and shade.
He speaks according to their capacity to hear.
Parables carry the public teaching while private explanation forms the disciples.
Evening comes and Jesus says to cross over.
They leave the crowd and take him in the boat.
A storm rises and fills the boat.
Jesus sleeps while fear asks whether he cares.
He speaks silence and stillness over wind and sea.
The storm exposes fear and undeveloped trust.
Awe replaces panic as they wonder who he is.
